# Genesis Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 13r–14r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_024_page_002.jpg`, `GEN_024_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0023 (page 19), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0024 (page 20), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0025 (page 21)

---

## A Wife for Isaac

**1** By this time Abraham was very old, and the LORD had blessed him in every way. **2** One day Abraham said to his most trusted servant — the one who managed everything he owned — "Put your hand under my thigh. **3** I want you to swear by the LORD, the God of heaven and earth, that you will not choose a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I live. **4** Instead, go back to my homeland, to my own relatives, and find a wife for my son Isaac."

**5** The servant asked him, "But what if the woman is unwilling to come back here with me? Should I take your son back to the land you came from?"

**6** "Absolutely not," Abraham told him. "Do not take my son back there. **7** The LORD, the God of heaven, who took me from my father's household and my native land, who spoke to me and swore to me, 'I will give this land to your offspring' — he will send his angel ahead of you, and you will find a wife for my son from there. **8** But if the woman refuses to come with you, then you are released from this oath. Only do not take my son back there." **9** So the servant placed his hand under Abraham's thigh and swore to carry out his master's wishes.

---

**10** The servant chose ten of his master's camels and set out, loaded with all kinds of valuable gifts from his master. He traveled to Aram-naharaim and made his way to the city of Nahor. **11** He had the camels kneel down by the well outside the city, near evening — the time when the women come out to draw water.

**12** Then he prayed: "LORD, God of my master Abraham, let things go well for me today, and show your faithful love to my master Abraham. **13** Here I am, standing beside this spring, and the young women of the town are coming out to draw water. **14** Let this be the sign: when I say to a girl, 'Please tilt your jar so I can drink,' and she says, 'Drink — and I'll water your camels too' — let her be the one you have chosen for your servant Isaac. That will be how I know you have shown faithful love to my master."

---

**15** Before he had even finished his prayer, a young woman appeared, coming out with a water jar on her shoulder. She was Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel son of Milcah — Milcah who was the wife of Abraham's brother Nahor. **16** She was strikingly beautiful — a virgin; no man had ever been with her. She came down to the spring, filled her jar, and came back up.

**17** The servant ran to meet her. "Please," he said, "could I have a little water from your jar?"

**18** "Of course, sir," she said. She quickly lowered the jar from her shoulder and let him drink. **19** When he had finished drinking, she said, "I'll draw water for your camels too, until they've had all they want." **20** She poured out what was left in her jar into the trough, hurried back to the well to draw more, and kept going until she had watered all his camels.

**21** All the while the servant watched her in wondering silence, trying to discern whether the LORD had made his journey successful or not.

---

**22** When the camels had finished drinking, the servant took out a gold nose ring weighing a beka, and two gold bracelets weighing ten shekels each, **23** and he asked her, "Whose daughter are you? Please tell me — is there room in your father's house for us to spend the night?"

**24** She answered, "I am the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah and Nahor." **25** And she added, "We have plenty of straw and fodder, and room to spend the night."

**26** The man bowed his head and worshiped the LORD. **27** "Praise be to the LORD," he said, "the God of my master Abraham, who has not abandoned his faithful love for my master! The LORD has led me straight to the house of my master's own relatives."

---

**28** The young woman ran home and told her mother's household what had happened. **29** Rebekah had a brother named Laban. He ran out to the man at the spring — **30** for he had seen the nose ring and the bracelets on his sister's wrists, and had heard her say, "This is what the man said to me." So Laban went out to the man and found him still standing by the camels at the spring. **31** "Come in," he said. "You are blessed by the LORD! Why are you standing out here? I've prepared the house, and there is room for the camels."

**32** So the man came inside. Laban unloaded the camels, provided straw and fodder, and brought water for the man and his companions to wash their feet. **33** Then food was set before him, but the servant said, "I cannot eat until I have told you why I've come."

"Go ahead," said Laban.

---

**34** So he said: "I am Abraham's servant. **35** The LORD has blessed my master enormously — he has become very wealthy. The LORD has given him flocks and herds, silver and gold, male and female servants, camels and donkeys. **36** Sarah, my master's wife, bore him a son in her old age, and my master has given him everything he owns. **37** My master made me swear an oath. He said, 'You are not to take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I live. **38** Instead, go to my father's family and my own clan, and find a wife for my son.' **39** I asked him, 'What if the woman will not come back with me?' **40** And he said, 'The LORD, in whose presence I have walked, will send his angel with you and make your journey a success. You will find a wife for my son from my own family, from my father's clan. **41** Only then will you be released from your oath — when you come to my people. If they refuse to give her to you, you are free from my oath.'

**42** "So today when I came to the spring, I prayed: 'LORD, God of my master Abraham, if you are truly going to make this journey of mine a success — **43** here I am, standing by the spring. When a young woman comes out to draw water, I will say to her: Please, give me a little water from your jar to drink. **44** And if she says, Both drink, and I'll draw water for your camels too — let her be the wife the LORD has chosen for my master's son.'

**45** "Before I had finished praying, Rebekah came out with her jar on her shoulder. She went down to the spring and drew water. I said to her, 'Please, give me a drink.' **46** She quickly lowered her jar from her shoulder and said, 'Drink, and I'll water your camels too.' So I drank, and she watered the camels as well.

**47** "Then I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?' She told me, 'I am the daughter of Bethuel son of Nahor and Milcah.' I put the ring on her nose and the bracelets on her wrists. **48** Then I bowed my head and worshiped the LORD. I praised the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who had led me on the right road to find a wife for my master's son from his own family.

**49** "Now then — if you intend to show faithful love and loyalty to my master, tell me. And if not, tell me that too, so I know which way to turn."

---

**50** Laban and Bethuel answered together: "This has clearly come from the LORD — there is nothing we can say, one way or another. **51** Here is Rebekah — take her and go. Let her be the wife of your master's son, just as the LORD has directed."

**52** When Abraham's servant heard their answer, he bowed down to the ground before the LORD. **53** Then he brought out gold and silver jewelry and fine clothing and gave them to Rebekah. He also gave costly gifts to her brother and her mother.

**54** After that, he and the men with him ate and drank and spent the night. In the morning he was up and ready. "Send me back to my master," he said.

**55** But Rebekah's brother and mother said, "Let the girl stay with us another ten days or so, and then you can go."

**56** "Please don't delay me," the servant said. "The LORD has made my journey a success — send me back so I can return to my master."

**57** "All right," they said, "let's call the girl and ask her what she wants to do." **58** They called Rebekah and asked her, "Will you go with this man?"

"I will go," she said.

---

**59** So they sent off their sister Rebekah with her nurse, along with Abraham's servant and his men. **60** And they blessed Rebekah with these words:

*"Our sister, may you become*
*the mother of thousands upon thousands.*
*May your children take possession*
*of the cities of those who hate them."*

**61** Then Rebekah and her young women packed up and mounted their camels and rode off with the man. So the servant took Rebekah and left.

---

**62** Now Isaac had come back from Beer-lahai-roi, for he was living in the Negev. **63** One evening he went out into the fields to walk and pray, and as he looked up, he saw camels approaching. **64** Rebekah also looked up, and when she saw Isaac, she climbed down from her camel. **65** She asked the servant, "Who is that man walking through the fields toward us?"

"That is my master," the servant answered.

So she took her veil and covered herself.

**66** The servant told Isaac everything that had happened. **67** Then Isaac brought Rebekah into the tent of his mother Sarah, and he married her. He loved her deeply, and she was a comfort to him after his mother's death.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 24 — the longest chapter in Genesis, and the greatest love story

Genesis 24 is the longest chapter in Genesis — 67 verses of narrative prose that functions as a complete short story. It has all the elements: a quest, a prayer, a test, a beautiful stranger at a well, a shrewd negotiation, a consent scene, and a meeting at dusk that closes the story with one of the most quietly devastating lines in scripture. It also contains one of the most sophisticated instances of narrative retelling in the Hebrew Bible: the servant's full recapitulation in verses 34-49 of events already narrated in verses 1-27, with significant variations, to show us how a wise man tells a story when persuasion is the goal.

---

### Verse 1 — "blessed in every way"

**וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אַבְרָהָם בַּכֹּל** — "and the LORD had blessed Abraham in everything." The Hebrew *bakkol* ("in everything/in all") is deliberately sweeping. The narrator signals from the opening line that this is a chapter about abundance and fulfillment. Everything Abraham has will be given to Isaac. Everything Isaac needs — a wife from the right family — is about to be found. The word *bakkol* appears again in verse 36 in the servant's retelling ("my master has given him *everything* he owns"), creating a bracket around the whole mission.

---

### Verse 2 — the oath on the thigh

**שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי** — "Put your hand under my thigh." This is the most intimate and solemn form of oath in the patriarchal world. The *yerekh* ("thigh") here refers to the generative organ — the seat of progeny and therefore the embodiment of everything at stake in this particular oath. Abraham is asking the servant to swear by the very source of lineage: find my son a wife, and let the future children of that marriage be the guarantee. The oath is confirmed in verse 9. I have rendered it plainly — "put your hand under my thigh" — without euphemism, because the text itself uses no euphemism. The physical gesture carries the full weight of what is being asked.

---

### Verse 7 — the angel ahead

**הוּא יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ לְפָנֶיךָ** — "He will send his angel before you." This is Abraham's confidence, not his instruction. He has lived long enough to know how the LORD works: the divine messenger goes ahead, prepares the way, and the human traveler walks into what has already been arranged. The angel is not visible in the story — there are no angelic appearances in Genesis 24. But the servant's prayer at the well and the extraordinary speed of its answer are the evidence that the angel has indeed gone before him.

---

### Verse 12 — "show your faithful love"

**עֲשֵׂה-חֶסֶד עִם-אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם** — "do *hesed* with my master Abraham." The word *hesed* (חֶסֶד) is one of the richest words in the Hebrew Bible and notoriously difficult to translate. It is covenantal love: loyalty, faithfulness, kindness, steadfast devotion — all of these together, within a relationship of committed obligation. Here the servant calls on God to act in accordance with the covenant he has made with Abraham. It is not a generic prayer for luck; it is a theologically specific appeal to the character of God as one who keeps promises. I have translated it "faithful love" throughout the chapter, though the full weight of *hesed* cannot be caught in any single English phrase.

---

### Verses 14/44 — the sign-test

The servant's sign is bold. He does not ask God to confirm the right woman by some private inner conviction. He asks for a public, observable, externally verifiable act: she must do what no stranger would normally do — offer to water ten camels. Ten camels coming off a long journey would drink approximately a hundred gallons of water. This would require many trips from the spring with a heavy jar. The test is for extraordinary generosity and extraordinary energy. It is, in short, a character test — the servant is asking God to reveal the woman's character through her action, not through supernatural intervention. Rebekah passes without being told she is being tested.

The slight variation in the servant's retelling (verse 43-44 vs. verses 13-14) is deliberate narrative art. In the prayer, the servant says "a young woman" (*ha-na'arah*); in the retelling, the same. But in the prayer the focus is on the sign itself; in the retelling the focus is on what happened. The servant is a careful rhetorician: when persuading Rebekah's family, he dwells on what the LORD *did* rather than on what he *planned*.

---

### Verse 21 — "watching her in wondering silence"

**וְהָאִישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ** — "And the man was *mishtaeh* toward her." This is one of the rarest verbs in the Hebrew Bible — the *hitpael* of **שָׁאָה** (*sha'ah*), occurring only here in this form. It carries the sense of gazing in silent astonishment or wonder, holding still while the mind works. The servant watches Rebekah work — jar after jar, trip after trip — in a state of suspended wonder. Is this the one? The text holds the moment open longer than the reader can stand. He does not yet know. He is watching, calculating, marveling. I have rendered this "watching her in wondering silence" to capture both the physical stillness and the inward intensity.

---

### Verse 22 — the gifts before the identity

One of the chapter's noted textual subtleties: the servant gives Rebekah the ring and bracelets *before* asking whose daughter she is (verse 22-23). In the retelling (verse 47), he reverses the order — as if he asked first, then gave the gifts. This is not a scribal error. It is a character portrait. In the original telling, the servant has already made up his mind — the sign has been given, this is the one — and gives the gifts in his excitement. In the retelling before Rebekah's family, he presents himself as having acted with greater deliberation (identifying her first, then giving gifts), because that version is more diplomatically reassuring. He is not lying; he is selecting. This is how real people tell stories when they need something.

---

### Verse 29-31 — Laban, the man who sees the jewelry

Laban appears here for the first time in the biblical narrative, and the narrator quietly establishes his character in a single clause: he saw the ring and the bracelets on his sister's wrists *and then* he ran out to invite the man in. We are meant to notice the order of events. Later, in Genesis 29-31, Laban will prove to be one of the great self-interested operators in the patriarchal stories. His character is introduced here with minimal commentary and maximum implication. The words he uses — "come in, you who are blessed by the LORD" — are impeccably hospitable; the motivation the narrator implies is a good deal more practical.

---

### Verses 34-49 — the art of retelling

The servant's recapitulation covers the same ground as verses 1-27 but with these significant shaping choices:

1. He opens with his identity ("I am Abraham's servant") and Abraham's wealth — because wealth establishes stakes.
2. He emphasizes that Abraham gave Isaac *everything* — raising the value of Isaac as a match.
3. He recounts the oath and the journey faithfully but in tighter form.
4. Crucially, in verse 47 he rearranges the gift-before-identity detail, as noted above — presenting it as identity-before-gift, which is the more proper sequence.
5. He closes with the direct question of verse 49: "If you intend to show faithful love and loyalty to my master, tell me." He has told the whole story as a single long argument whose conclusion is: you must say yes, because the LORD has already said yes.

This is rhetorical mastery embedded in narrative. The servant is not just describing what happened; he is constructing a case. The text honors this without comment, trusting the reader to see the craftsmanship.

---

### Verse 50 — "this has come from the LORD"

**מֵיְהוָה יָצָא הַדָּבָר** — "from the LORD this thing has gone out." Laban and Bethuel's answer is a theological concession before it is a social one. They are not just being polite; they are acknowledging that events have arranged themselves in a way that leaves no room for human resistance. The servant's sign, Rebekah's fulfillment of it, his prayer, her identity — the chain is too clean. "We can say nothing to you, either bad or good" — meaning: there is nothing left to negotiate. The LORD has spoken through events. This is the chapter's pivotal theological statement, offered by the most unexpected speakers: the girl's shrewd, jewelry-counting brother, and her father.

---

### Verse 58 — "I will go"

**וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלֵךְ** — "And she said: I will go."

Three words in Hebrew. Two in English. The shortest sentence in the chapter and the most important. The consent scene is framed carefully: Rebekah is called, asked, and answers for herself. She is not traded without consultation. In the ancient Near East, a woman could be betrothed by her father's and brother's authority alone — but the text takes care to record her own voice and her own choice. She has already acted with freedom and generosity at the well. Now she chooses her future with equal freedom. *Elekh* — "I will go." The same verb Abraham used in Genesis 12:1 when God told him to leave everything and go. Rebekah goes.

---

### Verse 60 — the blessing and its echo

**וְיִירַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ אֵת שַׁעַר שֹׂנְאָיו** — "and may your offspring possess the gate of those who hate them." The blessing given to Rebekah at her departure directly echoes the Abrahamic blessing given after the binding of Isaac in Genesis 22:17: "your offspring shall possess the gate of his enemies." The same language is now bestowed on the woman who will make that offspring possible. Rebekah carries the promise forward. The blessing her family gives her — without, presumably, knowing the Moriah oath — is the same covenant language. The narrative is knitting the strands together.

---

### Verse 62 — Beer-lahai-roi

**וְיִצְחָק בָּא מִבּוֹא בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי** — "Now Isaac had come from Beer-lahai-roi." Beer-lahai-roi is Hagar's well — "the well of the Living One who sees me" — where God appeared to Hagar in her abandonment (Genesis 16:14). Isaac has been at this place of exile and encounter. The narrator places him there without explanation. Is it a sign of Isaac's interiority — that he is drawn to places of divine encounter? Or is it simply a geographical note? The text does not say. But the choice of *this* well as Isaac's point of departure for meeting Rebekah is unlikely to be accidental.

---

### Verse 63 — *lasuach* in the field

**וַיֵּצֵא יִצְחָק לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה** — "And Isaac went out *lasuach* in the field." The verb **שִׂיחַ** (*siach*) in this *lamed*-infinitive form is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible in this usage. Its root carries the sense of speaking, musing, meditating, even complaining to God (cf. Psalm 77:4, 143:5). Ancient translators guessed at it: the Septuagint reads "to exercise" or "to walk" (*adolesxesai* — to engage in conversation, to talk to oneself); the Vulgate renders it *meditari* (to meditate); later tradition reads it as prayer. The honest answer is: we do not know exactly what Isaac was doing in that field at evening. He was doing something contemplative and solitary. I have translated "to walk and pray" to honor the most common interpretive tradition while acknowledging the openness of the word. What matters narratively is that he is alone, in the open field, in the evening light — and this is the moment he first sees her.

---

### Verse 64-65 — the veil

Rebekah sees Isaac and dismounts. She asks the servant who this man is, and when she learns it is her husband-to-be, she takes her veil and covers herself. The gesture of veiling is at once a social act (a bride's modesty protocol in this culture), a sign of transition (she is now in a new relational role), and a moment of extraordinary narrative restraint. We see them both from a distance, as if through the servant's eyes. They do not yet speak to each other. The chapter began with Abraham's oath and the servant's mission. It ends with two figures in a field at dusk, the story of how they were found having just been told, and the veil coming down.

---

### Verse 67 — "and she was a comfort to him"

**וַיִּנָּחֵם יִצְחָק אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ** — "and Isaac was comforted after his mother."

The word **נָחַם** (*nacham*) is the verb of comfort, consolation — the lifting of grief. Genesis 23 was Sarah's death chapter. Genesis 24 was the finding of Rebekah. And now, in the final clause, the narrator ties them together: Isaac brings Rebekah into his mother's tent, loves her, and is comforted. The grief of Genesis 23 finds its resolution here. The mother's tent — the space of Sarah's life and death — becomes the space of the new marriage. Isaac's love for Rebekah is given without elaborate description, but the emotional logic is placed plainly before us: he had been without his mother; now he has someone to love; the void is filled.

This is the only place in the entire patriarchal narrative where the Hebrew Bible explicitly says that a patriarch loved his wife at the time of marriage. Abraham's love for Sarah is shown through action; Jacob's love for Rachel is at first sight and then bitter labor. But here, in the final sentence of Genesis 24, the narrator simply says it: *he loved her.*

The story that began with an old man's dying wish — *do not let my son marry a Canaanite; go back to my people and find him a wife* — ends with the son standing in a field at dusk, watching camels arrive, and with the woman who will become the mother of the promised line, covered in her veil, riding toward him.

It is a beautiful story. It was meant to be.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 13r–14r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 24 begins in the right column of folio 13r (page 001 for this chapter), following the close of Genesis 23, and continues through folio 13v (page 002) and into folio 14r (page 003), concluding at verse 67 before Genesis 25 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 19–21 (pa_0023–pa_0025; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0023, /0024, and /0025). The Vaticanus LXX reads closely with the Hebrew throughout Genesis 24. Notable LXX variations: verse 21 — the LXX renders **mishtaeh** as **κατεμάνθανεν αὐτήν** ("was studying/observing her"), capturing the attentive watching but missing the element of astonished wonder in the Hebrew hapax; verse 47 — the LXX follows the retelling order (asks identity first, then gives gifts), perhaps harmonizing the narrative; verse 63 — the LXX reads **ἀδολεσχῆσαι** ("to converse/meditate aloud"), which interprets the hapax as a form of verbal musing. These variations confirm that the LXX translators were themselves grappling with the same difficult Hebrew constructions that challenge modern translators. No DSS fragments of Genesis 24 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
